content.text: Baseline Current-State Maturity Audit

The baseline maturity audit is the first structured assessment of where the Claude program stands across the six maturity dimensions. It is conducted once, near the beginning of the program (ideally at the end of Phase 1 or start of Phase 2), and establishes the baseline against which all future annual maturity assessments are compared. Without a baseline, maturity improvements are unmeasurable — and unmeasured improvements do not earn investment.

attrs.alt: Baseline maturity audit process — 6-week timeline with parallel workstreams

content.text: What Makes the Baseline Audit Different
Annual maturity assessments (conducted in subsequent years) benefit from institutional memory, established data collection processes, and known benchmarks. The baseline audit starts from zero:
No prior data to compare against:

the baseline must establish measurement methods that will be used consistently in future assessments. Design these methods carefully — changing the measurement approach between years makes year-on-year comparison unreliable.
Lower organizational awareness:

interviewees in a baseline audit may not have thought carefully about AI maturity before. Budget more time for context-setting and education, and fewer minutes for direct scoring questions, compared to a repeat assessment.
More resistance to honest scoring:

the first assessment carries more reputational risk for the people being assessed. An ESC member who scores poorly on Governance feels implicated in a way they won't feel after three years of improvement tracking. Manage this by emphasizing that the baseline's purpose is to direct investment, not to assign blame.
Higher discovery probability:

a baseline audit often surfaces AI use that the CoE is not aware of — shadow IT Claude deployments, personal API accounts, consumer tool use for work purposes. This discovery is valuable; plan for it and have a clear response policy ready.

The baseline audit's most important output is not the score — it is the investment plan that follows. A program that scores L1 across all dimensions and commits to a specific funded plan to reach L2 is in better shape than one that scores L2 but has no plan to reach L3.
The 6-Week Audit Process
Week 1: Scope and Planning
The first week is entirely preparation. No scoring or assessment work should begin before the scope is clear and the data collection infrastructure is in place.
Define audit scope:

which BUs, which systems, which geographic regions? A global organization auditing AI maturity across 20 countries faces a different challenge than one assessing a single headquarters operation. For a baseline audit, it is acceptable to scope to the primary site and 2–3 most active BUs, with other sites noted as out of scope and planned for year 2.
Establish the evidence standard:

define in advance what counts as evidence for each rubric criterion. 'An ESC has been established' requires: a list of named ESC members, evidence of at least one meeting (minutes or attendance record), and confirmation from the ESC Chair that the committee is active. Without an evidence standard, scores will vary based on how optimistic the assessor is.
Schedule all interviews:

book all interviews in week 1, to be conducted in weeks 3 and 4. Interview scheduling is the most common cause of audit delays. Get calendar commitments from senior stakeholders while the ESC Chair's sponsorship is fresh.
Launch employee survey:

send the 5-question culture survey at the end of week 1 so responses accumulate through weeks 2–3. The survey goes to a stratified sample: 20–30% of each target BU, including both known Claude users and likely non-users.
Week 2: Data Review

Data review assembles the documentary evidence for each dimension before interviews begin. This ensures interviewers are informed, not blank — they can confirm or challenge what the data suggests rather than starting from zero.
Dimension
Documents to Review
Metrics to Pull
Strategy & Governance
ESC charter, decision rights doc, phase gate approval records, CoE org chart
ESC meeting attendance rate, phase gate on-time rate, decision log entry count
Talent & Capability
CoE job descriptions, champion list, training completion records, skills assessment results
CoE team headcount, trained users by BU, champion activation rate
Use Case Portfolio
Portfolio register, intake log, tier classification records, kill/sunset log
Total use cases by tier and status, average time from intake to production, kill rate
Platform & Technology
API gateway architecture doc, security assessment, monitoring dashboard, infrastructure runbook
API usage by use case, error rates, uptime, cost by use case
Data & Knowledge
Data classification policy, approved data source registry, RAG index coverage doc
Data sources accessible per use case type, PII incidents from Claude, data quality defect rate
Culture & Adoption
Training completion records, satisfaction survey results, onboarding checklist
Monthly active users, new user growth rate, training completion by BU, override rate

When a document does not exist that the rubric expects to find at a given level, that is evidence in itself: it suggests the organization may not be at the level where that document should exist. Note 'document not found' as a data point, not just a gap.
Weeks 3–4: Structured Interviews
Interviews generate qualitative evidence that documents cannot provide: perception, context, and what is not written down. Run 20-minute interviews with the 18–24 stakeholders identified in Week 1.
Interview Question Bank by Dimension
Use these questions consistently across interviews for the same dimension to enable comparison:

# STRATEGY & GOVERNANCE — Interview Questions

1. "What decisions about the Claude program do you personally make?
   What decisions require someone else's approval?"
   [Tests decision rights clarity]

2. "If you disagreed with a CoE decision about a use case in your BU,
   what would you do?"
   [Tests conflict resolution mechanism]

3. "Has the Claude program's governance ever gotten in the way of something
   that should have been faster? Give me a specific example."
   [Tests governance friction vs. value]

4. "Who in your organization is accountable for the Claude program's
   success? How do you know?"
   [Tests accountability clarity]

---

# TALENT & CAPABILITY — Interview Questions

1. "If you needed help building a Claude use case next week, who would
   you call? How confident are you they could help?"
   [Tests talent accessibility]

2. "What AI skills do you wish your team had that they currently don't?"
   [Tests capability gaps from the demand side]

3. "If the CoE team were unavailable for 3 months, what would happen
   to your BU's Claude use cases?"
   [Tests CoE dependency vs. BU self-sufficiency]

---

# CULTURE & ADOPTION — Interview Questions

1. "When you're facing a task at work, does Claude come to mind as a
   possible tool? Walk me through an example."
   [Tests habitual vs. deliberate use]

2. "What would need to change for you to use Claude more than you do now?"
   [Tests adoption barriers]

3. "Do you know anyone in your team who avoids Claude? What's their
   concern?"
   [Tests resistance patterns]
Week 5: Analysis and Scoring
With data review complete and interviews conducted, the CoE Director scores each dimension using the rubric. The scoring process:
For each rubric criterion at each level, list the evidence that confirms or contradicts the criterion being met
Score the dimension at the highest level where all criteria are confirmed by evidence
For any criterion not confirmed by evidence, note it as a gap (this drives the investment plan)
Calculate confidence in each score: High (multiple independent evidence sources confirm), Medium (some evidence, some inference), Low (primarily inference or single source)
Flag any scores where data review and interviews diverged — these deserve explicit attention in the analysis and may indicate organizational perception gaps

When interviews and data diverge — for example, documents show a data classification policy exists but interviewees say they have never seen it or do not follow it — score at the lower level. Policy existence without policy practice is not maturity.
Week 6: ESC Readout and Investment Commitment
The baseline audit culminates in an ESC readout. The readout's goal is not just to present findings — it is to secure a funded investment commitment to address the binding constraint:
Open with the overall maturity level and the binding constraint — this should be the first thing the ESC sees, not buried in slide 5

Present each dimension score with 2–3 pieces of specific evidence (not 'governance is weak' but 'the ESC has met twice but made no binding decisions — all decisions in the decision log were made by the CoE Director alone')
Show the investment required to close the binding constraint gap, with cost and timeline
Close with a specific ask: 'I am requesting approval for [investment] to address the binding constraint in [dimension]. This will move us from L[X] to L[X+1] within 12 months.'
Get a signed commitment (or ESC meeting minutes with a formal resolution) before the meeting ends
Handling Uncomfortable Findings
Baseline audits routinely surface uncomfortable findings. The CoE Director must be prepared to present these without softening them into meaninglessness:
Shadow IT Discovery

The most common uncomfortable finding: employees are using consumer Claude.ai, personal API accounts, or unauthorized AI tools for work. This is both a security risk and a signal that official channels are insufficient.
Do not frame this as an employee discipline issue — frame it as a supply-demand mismatch: users have AI needs that the official program is not meeting
Present it to the ESC as a risk and an opportunity: the risk is ungoverned data in consumer tools; the opportunity is that demonstrated demand validates the investment case
Recommend a response: provide governed access quickly rather than issuing a prohibition that will simply drive shadow use further underground
Governance-Reality Gap

Documents say one thing; practice says another. The ESC charter describes a monthly meeting; records show 3 meetings in 12 months. The data classification policy is comprehensive; no use case builder has read it.
Present the gap directly: 'Our documents suggest L2 on Governance, but the evidence of practice suggests L1. Here is the specific gap.'
Avoid attributing blame — 'the ESC has met infrequently' rather than 'the ESC Chair has failed to call meetings'
The governance-reality gap is itself a finding worth communicating: if governance exists on paper but not in practice, the program is at risk of believing it has protections it does not have
Talent Overconcentration
AI capability is concentrated in 1–3 people who are the CoE team. If they leave, the program loses most of its technical capacity. This is common in early-stage programs and must be surfaced:
Quantify the concentration: 'Three people currently hold 80% of the AI engineering knowledge in the organization. The departure of any one of them would delay our Q2 milestone by at least 2 months.'

Present it as a risk requiring mitigation, not just an aspiration to hire more people: knowledge documentation, cross-training, and structured onboarding plans for new CoE staff are concrete responses
Baseline Audit Report Structure
The written baseline audit report is delivered to the ESC and archived as the program's founding governance document. It should be 15–25 pages:

# Baseline Maturity Audit Report — Outline

Executive Summary (1 page)
  - Overall maturity level and binding constraint
  - Top 3 findings
  - Investment recommendation summary

Audit Scope and Methodology (1–2 pages)
  - What was included and excluded
  - Data sources reviewed
  - Interviewees (listed by role, not name, to encourage candor)
  - Scoring methodology and evidence standards

Dimension-by-Dimension Findings (8–12 pages, ~1.5 pages per dimension)
  For each dimension:
    - Current score with rationale
    - Evidence summary (2–3 strongest evidence items)
    - Gap to next level
    - Investment required to close the gap

Cross-Cutting Findings (2–3 pages)
  - Maturity imbalances identified
  - Shadow IT / ungoverned AI use findings
  - Organizational risk factors
  - Positive signals and existing strengths to build on

Investment Plan (2–3 pages)
  - Binding constraint and proposed investment
  - Dimension-by-dimension 12-month targets
  - Budget requirements
  - Timeline and milestones

Appendix
  - Full evidence log (all rubric criteria with evidence)
  - Survey results summary
  - Interview question bank used
Using the Baseline for Future Assessments
The baseline audit's lasting value is as the foundation for year-on-year progress tracking. To enable this:
Archive the evidence log:

the detailed evidence log for each criterion is the foundation for next year's comparison. Future assessors can see exactly what evidence was used in year 1 and use the same evidence types in year 2 for a like-for-like comparison.
Document the scoring decisions:

when scores were assigned at a lower level than documents suggested (due to governance-practice gaps or other inconsistencies), document this reasoning. Future assessors should understand why a score was assigned, not just what it was.
Set explicit targets:

at the ESC readout, agree on specific score targets for each dimension at the next assessment. 'By next year's assessment, we target L3 on Platform and L3 on Culture' creates accountability and a clear success criterion.
Repeat the same survey:

use the same 5-question employee survey each year. Small changes in question wording invalidate year-on-year comparison. Even if a question could be improved, consistency is more valuable than perfection.
Employee Survey Design
The 5-question employee survey is the primary instrument for measuring Culture and Adoption maturity. Its design determines whether results are actionable or ambiguous.
Survey Instrument

# Claude Adoption Baseline Survey

Instructions: This survey takes 3 minutes. Responses are anonymous.
Results will be used to understand how Claude AI tools can be better
supported across the organization.

Q1. How would you describe your current use of Claude at work?
  (a) I use Claude tools daily or weekly
  (b) I use Claude tools occasionally (a few times a month)
  (c) I have tried Claude tools but rarely use them
  (d) I have not used Claude tools at work
  (e) I was not aware Claude tools were available for work use

Q2. If you use Claude tools: on average, how much time per week do
   they save you? (Skip if Q1 = d or e)
  (a) More than 2 hours
  (b) 1–2 hours
  (c) 30–60 minutes
  (d) Less than 30 minutes

Q3. What is the biggest barrier to using Claude tools more in your work?
   (Select up to 2)
  □ I don't know how to use them effectively
  □ I'm not sure which tasks they would help with
  □ I don't trust the accuracy of the output
  □ Using them feels slower than doing the task myself
  □ I'm concerned about data privacy or security
  □ My manager or team hasn't encouraged it
  □ The tools are hard to access
  □ I don't have any barriers — I already use them regularly
  □ Other: ___________________

Q4. Have you completed any Claude training offered by the organization?
  (a) Yes, and I found it useful
  (b) Yes, but I didn't find it very useful
  (c) No, but I'd like to
  (d) No, and I don't think I need it
  (e) I wasn't aware training was available

Q5. If you could change one thing about how the organization supports
   AI tools at work, what would it be?
  [Free text — 1–3 sentences]
Distribute the survey to a stratified sample: aim for at least 30 respondents per BU and a response rate of 40%+. Follow up with a reminder at day 7 if response rate is below 25%.
Interpreting Survey Results
Map survey responses to maturity level indicators:
Survey Metric
L1 Indicator
L2 Indicator
L3 Indicator
L4 Indicator
Q1: Usage frequency (a+b)
< 10% daily/weekly users
10–30% daily/weekly users
30–60% daily/weekly users
> 60% daily/weekly users
Q1: Awareness (not (e))
< 50% aware Claude is available
50–70% aware
70–90% aware
> 90% aware
Q4: Training completion (a+b)
< 20% completed training
20–40% completed
40–70% completed
> 70% completed
Q3: Top barrier
'Don't know how to use' or 'Not aware'
'Don't know which tasks' or 'Hard to access'
'Don't trust accuracy'
'No barriers' or capability-specific concerns

Q5 (free text) often yields the highest-quality insights. Read every response. Common themes become data points: if 30% of respondents mention that their manager has never mentioned Claude, that is a Culture dimension finding that interviews may not surface.
Audit Failure Modes: What Goes Wrong
Baseline audits fail in predictable ways. Knowing the failure modes in advance allows the CoE Director to design against them:
Score inflation:

the organization consistently rates itself higher than evidence supports. Common in organizations with a culture of positive self-reporting. Mitigation: require specific evidence for every score above L1; audit the evidence, not the claims.
Interview monoculture:

the CoE Director only interviews people who are enthusiastic about Claude, producing a sample biased toward optimistic assessments. Mitigation: specifically recruit 2–3 skeptics or non-users for interviews; their perspective is the most valuable for identifying real barriers.
Data unavailability treated as L1:

if a metric (e.g., monthly active users) cannot be measured because the monitoring infrastructure does not exist, this is treated as evidence that the organization is at L1 on Platform maturity — even if excellent monitoring exists but is not yet accessible to the auditor. Mitigation: distinguish between 'metric does not exist' (L1 evidence) and 'metric exists but was not accessible during the audit' (needs follow-up, not a default L1 score).
Binding constraint misidentification:

the team focuses on the dimension that is easiest to improve rather than the one that is actually limiting overall maturity. Mitigation: run the binding constraint analysis mechanically — the lowest-scoring dimension is the binding constraint, regardless of how hard it is to improve.
Report without commitment:

the audit produces a thorough report that is presented to the ESC, acknowledged, and filed without producing a funded investment decision. Mitigation: do not accept 'we'll think about it' as an outcome of the ESC readout. Request a specific decision — approve the investment, modify it, or explicitly defer it with a date for revisiting.
Summary

The baseline current-state maturity audit is a 6-week process that establishes the program's starting point across all six maturity dimensions. It combines documentary evidence review, structured stakeholder interviews, and employee survey data to produce dimension scores, identify the binding constraint, and generate an investment plan. Uncomfortable findings — shadow IT, governance-practice gaps, talent concentration — must be presented honestly to have governance value. The written report is 15–25 pages and serves as the founding governance document. Its evidence log, scoring decisions, and survey instrument are archived to enable like-for-like year-on-year comparison in future annual assessments.
